lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing repeat outsourced geophysical data acquisition, processing, interpretation, and mapping for resource, engineering, environmental, and public-sector customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive units, shells, controlled financing vehicles, owned-data-library businesses, standalone software publishing or custom software development, equipment resale, and training businesses are excluded.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External customers buy accountable field and technical services on repeat programs or discrete contracted assignments; revenue is principally service revenue rather than software licenses or equipment margin.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code's product list includes service work alongside standalone software, equipment resale, and training. The lens retains repeat outsourced geophysical service providers because those businesses share field-data and professional-interpretation delivery economics; the excluded activities do not.

a.rationale: I estimate that AI can substitute or avoid hiring for a meaningful share of data conditioning, classification, map production, reporting, proposal preparation, and first-pass interpretation. The occupation evidence also shows physical collection, instrument operation, accuracy checks, field judgment, and expert interpretation, which keeps most current labor outside the exposed share.
a.change_evidence: A firm-level wage-and-hours task inventory covering field crews, processors, geophysicists, project managers, and back office; paired pilots measuring labor hours before and after AI-assisted processing and reporting.
caveats: No published wage-weighted occupation mix was located for the narrowed six-digit lens.
O*NET occupation evidence spans employers outside this industry.
The estimate concerns current tasks exposed to substitution or avoided hiring, not achieved savings.

rho.rationale: I estimate only part of the exposed work will be operational within the horizon because field data, uncertain subsurface inference, customer acceptance, documented methods, and quality control require human review. The USGS evidence shows active AI transition in comparable assessment workflows, supporting implementation above a purely experimental level.
rho.change_evidence: Customer acceptance criteria, professional-liability and quality-control policies, production-system audit trails, and measured adoption from geophysical service providers using AI-assisted interpretation.
caveats: USGS work is a public-sector adjacent workflow, not an observed adoption rate for lower-middle-market vendors.
Implementation varies sharply by modality, customer criticality, and data quality.

e.rationale: I estimate that about half of the injected lower-middle-market count fits the narrowed service lens. The official product list explicitly associates consulting, training, custom software, software publishing, and equipment resale with this code, while the NAICS definition centers geophysical data services; this creates material uncertainty in the service-only share.
e.change_evidence: A purchased business-register extract with product descriptions, brokered-company financial statements, and interviews with owners to separate outsourced service revenue from software, resale, and training revenue.
caveats: The product list identifies products associated with the code but does not publish the fraction of firms or receipts in each activity.
The injected firm count is not re-estimated here.

s5.rationale: I estimate a modest qualifying-transfer probability because a specialist professional-service firm can be saleable when client relationships, staff, equipment, and backlog transfer, but owner expertise and cyclical resource exposure can prevent a control sale. The broad small-business market shows continuing completed service-sector transactions, but it is not an industry transfer-rate measure.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed-deal data from GF Data, PitchBook, or a specialist energy-services broker; owner-age and succession data for firms in this code; and a review of asset-sale versus stock-sale outcomes.
caveats: No observed five-year control-transfer rate was located for eligible firms in this six-digit industry.
The cited marketplace data is broad and skews toward businesses smaller than the target EBITDA band.

q.rationale: I estimate limited retention of implemented gross benefit. Service buyers can seek lower bids, require price reductions at renewal, or bring more desktop work inside, while field execution, domain expertise, data provenance, and accountable sign-off leave some retained margin for an efficient operator.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts and bid tabs showing price-adjustment clauses, hourly versus fixed-fee mix, renewal win rates, post-automation realized pricing, and customer interviews on savings-sharing expectations.
caveats: No public contract-level pricing or margin-pass-through dataset was located for the narrowed lens.
The value retained can differ materially between government, mineral, engineering, and energy customers.

d5.rationale: I estimate roughly flat constant-price, constant-quality service demand at year five. Public critical-mineral mapping supports demand for geophysical work, while the national energy outlook projects lower US crude production through the mid-2030s in most cases, creating downside for resource-exploration assignments. Adjacent mapping occupations have a positive long-run employment outlook, but that is not an industry demand forecast.
d5.change_evidence: Revenue and backlog by end market from the Census Annual Integrated Economic Survey, state and federal geophysical procurement awards, exploration budgets, and customer-level renewal data.
caveats: Government mapping funding and national oil-production projections are drivers, not a measured forecast for this service basket.
The BLS occupation outlook is a different population and a longer horizon.
Demand is exposed to commodity prices, mineral policy, permitting, and capital budgets.

o.rationale: I estimate most remaining demand still needs an accountable operator. Field acquisition, instrument handling, safety, survey design, validation, and interpretation of uncertain subsurface conditions remain operator-required even as software automates portions of processing and mapping.
o.change_evidence: A modality-level review of customer scopes of work, remote-operation adoption, professional sign-off requirements, and software-versus-service spending at repeat customers.
caveats: O*NET tasks describe adjacent occupations rather than the narrowed firm population.
Some desktop interpretation and map production could be supplied entirely by software faster than assumed.

narrative.executive_view: The narrowed industry supplies specialized outsourced geophysical field and interpretation services. It has credible AI-enabled workflow improvement, but its small addressable service-only population, end-market cyclicality, and uncertain price retention warrant careful diligence.
narrative.ai_impact: AI is most useful for data conditioning, pattern recognition, map and report production, and first-pass interpretation. O*NET task evidence also points to field acquisition, physical instruments, accuracy checks, technical judgment, and client communication that constrain full substitution. USGS is actively applying machine learning to accelerate comparable mineral-assessment work.
narrative.value_capture: The benefit is likely to appear as faster turnaround and lower internal production effort, but buyers can demand some of that benefit through competitive bids or repricing. Retention should be tested from actual contracts, bid tabs, and renewal outcomes rather than inferred from technical feasibility.
narrative.firm_availability: The code contains more than the target service model: Census product materials associate it with consulting, training, software, and equipment resale as well as geophysical work. Eligible firms may transfer, but specialist relationships, key-person expertise, equipment condition, and exposure to resource cycles can limit a qualifying sale.
narrative.demand_durability: Critical-mineral mapping provides a positive public-demand channel, including USGS investment in airborne geophysical surveys. Counterbalancing that support, EIA expects lower US crude production through the mid-2030s in most cases, so demand from exploration-oriented customers is not durable by default.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest unknowns are the share of firms that are true repeat outsourced providers, their revenue mix by end market, the share of work that remains field-bound, and contract-level ability to retain efficiency gains. Commodity cycles, procurement concentration, professional liability, data quality, and customer acceptance can each change the result materially.
narrative.principal_driver: AI-assisted data processing and interpretation can reduce labor required per delivered geophysical project while preserving accountable field and expert review.
narrative.principal_weakness: No direct six-digit evidence was located for the service-only firm share, qualifying control-transfer rate, contract pass-through, or five-year demand for the frozen lens.

sources.title: Census NAICS industry definition for 541360
sources.supports: Defines 541360 as establishments gathering, interpreting, and mapping geophysical data, often for subsurface resources or engineering surveys.

sources.title: Census NAPCS product list for NAICS 54136
sources.supports: Lists associated geophysical consulting, training, custom software, published software, and equipment-resale products alongside geophysical services.

sources.title: O*NET Surveying and Mapping Technicians
sources.supports: Describes field measurement, instrument operation, GIS entry, map production, calculations, accuracy checks, and drone image capture tasks.

sources.title: O*NET Geoscientists, Except Hydrologists and Geographers
sources.supports: Describes data analysis, geological interpretation, resource location, reports, field inspection, accuracy, and technical skills for the relevant professional occupation.

sources.title: USGS machine-learning work for critical-mineral assessments
sources.supports: Reports that CriticalMAAS is developing machine-learning tools to accelerate staff-intensive data compilation and analysis for critical-mineral assessments.

sources.title: USGS critical-minerals mapping investment
sources.supports: Reports USGS investment in new airborne geophysical surveys for critical-mineral resource identification.

sources.title: BizBuySell small-business transaction report
sources.supports: Reports continued completed small-business transactions and growth in service-sector transaction volume during 2025.

sources.title: EIA Annual Energy Outlook narrative
sources.supports: States that in EIA projections Brent prices remain below a specified threshold through 2030 and US crude production declines through the mid-2030s in nearly all cases.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook for Cartographers and Photogrammetrists
sources.supports: Reports projected employment growth for cartographers and photogrammetrists and describes their mapping work.
